text:
The evolution of Generative Pre-Trained Transformers (GPT) and large language models (LLMs) has raised academic concerns worldwide, and there is a global search for reliable ways to identify the AI-generated content. In this study, we investigate the effectiveness of current AI detection tools. Our aim is to assess their ability to reliably differentiate between human written content and content generated by ChatGPT, and to examine the effect of machine translation and manual obfuscation on the performance of current AI detection tools.

This research examined 14 platforms consisting of 12 publicly available and two commercial end user applications (Turnitin and PlagiarismCheck). The results of the experiments show that currently, none of the applications achieve the required accuracy and reliability standards for high-stakes academic applications. There is a large bias towards labeling AI-generated content as content that was produced by humans and this bias was even larger when the content was obfuscated.

This paper makes three contributions to the research area. First, it offers a summary of the current state-of-the-art in the scientific literature and industry tools for detecting AI-generated content. Second, it includes a rigorous empirical analysis using a novel large-scale corpus and a wide range of detection tools. Third, it engages with the limitations and ethics of deploying AI-detection tools in academic settings.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
